coredata.dc:title: Dual-stage amplified fluorescent DNA sensor based on polymerase-Mediated strand displacement reactions
coredata.dc:creator: He, Shuyi
Li, Peng
Tang, Le
Chen, Mingjian
Yang, Yuxin
Zeng, Zhaoyang
Xiong, Wei
Wu, Xu
Huang, Jiahao
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive fluorescent strategy for DNA detection is developed based on a dual stage amplification process mediated by DNA polymerase. In the sensing method, there are two kinds of DNA probes used, including a hairpin probe (HP) and a double-stranded DNA (DSD) tagged with a quencher and a fluorophore, respectively. The presence of the target DNA triggers the two-step cyclic amplification reactions with the assistance of DNA polymerase, thus leading to the considerable fluorescence restoration. The experiment conditions are carefully studied to achieve optimal performance, including the construction of DSD, the concentration of HP, the reaction time, and the amounts of polymerase. Under the optimal conditions, the strategy responded linearly to DNA target from 0.5 to 50.0 nM with a relative low detection limit of 0.38 nM (according to 3σ/S rule). Moreover, the method shows good detection performance in serum samples, confirming its practical usage for efficient DNA assay. The mix-and-measure simplicity and dual-stage signal amplification make the developed method a novel way for sensitive detection of DNA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA assay
Dual signal enhancement
Strand displacement reaction
Polymerase
Fluorescence quenching and recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility investigation and condition optimization of the sensing method
DNA detection in aqueous buffer solution and diluted fetal bovine serum
Construction and operation of the established fluorescent DNA sensor
Optimization of the DNA detection system
Assay performance of the DNA sensing platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant Nos. 21806186 , 21975117 , 21705076 , 31741049 , and CSU Innovation-Driven funding ( 20180026040002 ), Huxiang High-Level Talent Gathering Project -innovative talents ( 2019RS1005 ) and the Research Innovation Program for Graduates of Central South University ( 1053320190609 and 202211901 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2020JJ4766 , 2020JJ4125 and 2018JJ3815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Feasibility investigation of the proposed DNA sensing system. (A) The fluorescence spectra of the mixture of DNA1, DNA2, and HP (c) upon the introduction of polymerases and dNTPs (a) and further with the addition of target DNA (b). (B) The polyacrylamind gel electrophoresis results. Lane 1: DNA1 (1 μM); Lane 2: DNA2 (1 μM); Lane 3: DNA1 (1 μM) + DNA2 (1 μM); Lane 4: HP (1.5 μM); Lane 5: DNA1 (1 μM) + DNA2 (1 μM) + HP (1.5 μM); Lane 6: Target DNA (500 nM); Lane 7: Target DNA (500 nM) + HP (1.5 μM); Lane 8: DNA1 (1 μM) + DNA2 (1 μM) + HP (1.5 μM) + Target DNA (500 nM); Lane 9: DNA1 (1 μM) + DNA2 (1 μM) + HP (1.5 μM) + Polymerase (20 U/mL); Lane 10: DNA1 (1 μM) + DNA2 (1 μM) + HP (1.5 μM) + Polymerase (20 U/mL) + Target DNA (500 nM). The red, blue, and green arrows indicate the bands for signaling DNA products (SDP), Target-HP complex, and DNA1, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optimization of experimental conditions. (A) The effect of DNA2 length. (B) The impact of ratios of DNA2 over DNA1. (C) The effect of HP concentration. (D) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the reaction time. (E) The effect of the amounts of polymerase.

Sensitivity study. (A) Fluorescence spectra of the sensor system challenged with the addition of target DNA with concentration varying from 0.1 to 100 nM. (B) The dependence of the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm on the target concentrations between 0.5 and 50 nM.

Specificity examination. Relative fluorescence response for different DNA sequences. All the samples were reacted at 37 ℃ for 1 h after the addition of 100 nM different DNA sequences, including the perfectly matched DNA target, 1 M, 3 M, and random sequences.

Schematic illustration of the dual-recycling DNA amplified detection system. The presence of target DNA bind and open the hairpin probe (HP) (step i), and then form target-HP complex. Upon the introduction of double-stranded DNA (DSD) (step ii) which exhibits an initially quenched fluorescence signal, the target-HP complex hybridizes with DNA2. The addition of polymerase (step iii) triggers the strand extension with the help of dNTPs. As a result, both the target (step iv) and DNA1 (step v) are liberated, which participate the cycle I and cycle II, respectively. Moreover, the generation of signaling DNA product (SDP) keeps the dye-tagged DNA2 away from the quencher-tethered DNA1, thus leading to a fluorescence recovery. In the reaction cycle II, the released DNA1 hybridizes with HP (step vi), the exposed part of which then binds to DSD (vii). The presence of polymerase (step viii) is able to launch base incorporation and strand displacement, thereby resulting in the detachment of DNA1 (ready for the next reaction round) and SDP (ready for fluorescence measurement) (step ix).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Polymerase-aided, hairpin DNA-based, signal-amplified nucleic acid testing methods.
Determination of DNA in fetal bovine serum samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-stage amplified fluorescent DNA sensor based on polymerase-Mediated strand displacement reactions

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive fluorescent strategy for DNA detection is developed based on a dual stage amplification process mediated by DNA polymerase. In the sensing method, there are two kinds of DNA probes used, including a hairpin probe (HP) and a double-stranded DNA (DSD) tagged with a quencher and a fluorophore, respectively. The presence of the target DNA triggers the two-step cyclic amplification reactions with the assistance of DNA polymerase, thus leading to the considerable fluorescence restoration. The experiment conditions are carefully studied to achieve optimal performance, including the construction of DSD, the concentration of HP, the reaction time, and the amounts of polymerase. Under the optimal conditions, the strategy responded linearly to DNA target from 0.5 to 50.0 nM with a relative low detection limit of 0.38 nM (according to 3σ/S rule). Moreover, the method shows good detection performance in serum samples, confirming its practical usage for efficient DNA assay. The mix-and-measure simplicity and dual-stage signal amplification make the developed method a novel way for sensitive detection of DNA.

ce:section.ce:para: With the completion of the Human Genome Project and the development of genome technology, sequence-specific oligonucleotide detection has drawn an increasingly growing interest [1,2]. Recent research reports have shown that DNA detection can be applied not only in the food safety [3], as well as pharmacogenomics [4], but also can be employed in plasma and other biological samples to achieve reliable diagnosis of virus-induced diseases [5] or cancer [6]. In the past ten years, traditional methods for DNA assay have been developed based on changes of the mass [7], fluorescence [8], electrochemical features [9], resonance light scattering [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [11], surface plasmon resonance [12], electrochemiluminescence [13], colorimetry [14], and so forth. Among them, fluorescence detection is of great importance due to its low cost, high sensitivity, and facile operation [15].

The conventional methods applied for nucleic acid analysis are polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and microarray technology [16]. However, these methods are costly, complicated, and time-consuming [17]. Therefore, a variety of inexpensive, facile, and efficient DNA sensing strategies have been reported for nucleic acid detection. For example, single-stranded probes [18], binary probes [19], displacement probes [20], and hairpin probes (HP) [21] have been proposed [22]. Nanomaterials are also widely utilized in combination with these DNA probes due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties, such as gold nanoparticles [23], silver nanoclusters [24], carbon nanoparticles [25], carbon nanotubes [26], silicon nanowires [27], graphene [28], and molybdenum disulfide [29]. But the potential toxicity and tedious synthesis procedures of nanomaterials greatly constraint their popular applications. Consequently, it is highly desired to construct robust sensing schemes without the employment of nanomaterials while maintaining the essential merits, including substantial signal enhancement and easy-to-implement manipulation.

In this work, we report a low-cost, fast, sensitive, and accurate DNA detection method based on a DNA polymerase-assisted dual amplification system. The sensing system employs an HP, a double-strand DNA (DSD) labeled with a quencher and a fluorophore, and DNA polymerase. In the presence of target DNA, a dual cyclic amplification process was triggered to form a large number of signaling DNA products (SDPs), which results in significant fluorescence enhancement. The approach is promising with several intriguing features. First, there is only one kind of enzyme, polymerase, used in the proposed method, which is completely different from previously reported schemes, in which polymerases, nicking enzymes and CRISPR/Cas9 nucleases are demanded [30–33]. Furthermore, the operation of this sensor is simple, since there is only one step required: mix-and-measure. Moreover, the assay is rapid, since there is no any extra measure to be performed, such as washing and separation processes. Additionally, the detection sensitivity is expected to be remarkably amplified due to the involvement of two-stage signal enhancement mechanism and an efficiently quenched background signal. All these advantages make it possible to provide a powerful platform for DNA detection.

The quencher and fluorophore-labeled DNA probes (DNA1, DNA2-A, DNA2-B, DNA2-C) and label-free hairpin probes (HP) were purchased from Takara Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Takara, China). And the target DNA, single-base mismatched DNA (1M), triple-base mismatched DNA (3M), and random DNA were all purchased from Life Technologies Co., Ltd. (China). Their sequences are listed in Table 1 . Klenow fragment, NEB Buffer 2, and dNTPs were purchased from New England Biolabs (NEB, Ipswich, US). DMSO (Dimethyl sulfoxide) and acrylamide were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (USA). Tris, persulfate amine, TEMED, boric acid, EDTA, and NaOH were obtained from Ameresco Corporation (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Gibco Life Technologies (USA). Super GelRed® was obtained from US Everbright Inc (China). Purified water provided by Milli-Q (Millipore, USA) was used to prepare all solutions. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan). Samples were incubated in a DC-0506 water bath (Qianjun science instrument, Shanghai) at 37 ℃. Images of polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) were taken under Gel Doc XR System image analysis system (Bio-Rad, USA).

A 100 μL aliquot of sample was hold in a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by exciting at 496 nm and the intensity was measured between 510 nm and 650 nm. Both excitation and emission slits were set at 5 nm. The experimental data was analyzed by using the software GraphPad Prism 6 (GraphPad, LaJolla, CA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility investigation and condition optimization of the sensing method
ce:section.ce:para: 2.0 μL 10 μM DNA1 and 2.0 μL 10 μM DNA2-A were added in a 91.6 μL reaction buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 100 mM dNTPs, 6% DMSO, and 1 mM DTT, pH 8.0) and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Afterwards, 3.0 vL 10 μM HP and 0.4 μL 5000 U/mL polymerase were introduced into the solution. Finally, 1.0 μL 10 μM target DNA or ultra-pure water was introduced into the system to initiate the reaction at 37 ℃ for another 60 min. The fluorescence spectra were then measured with an excitation wavelength fixed at 496 nm. Then, the length of DNA-2, the ratio between DNA-1 and DNA-2, concentrations of HP and polymerase, and the reaction time were all carefully investigated.

The detection sensitivity of the DNA sensor was evaluated. The mixture containing 2.0 μL 1 μM DNA1 and 2.0 μL 1 μM DNA2-A were added in a 91.6 μL reaction buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 100 mM dNTPs, 6% DMSO, and 1 mM DTT, pH 8.0) and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Then, 3.0 μL 10 μM HP and 0.4 μL 500 U/mL polymerase were added to the solution. Afterwards, a series of target DNA with different concentrations were added into the solution and incubated for another 60 min at 37 ℃. The final concentrations of target DNA in the solution were 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 50.0, and 100.0 nM, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of each solution were scanned with the excitation wavelength of 496 nm and recorded from 510 nm to 650 nm.

The sensing selectivity over mismatched DNA probes were also examined. 2.0 μL 10 μM DNA1 and 2.0 μL 10 μM DNA2-A were mixed together in a 91.6 μL reaction buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 100 mM dNTPs, 6% DMSO, and 1 mM DTT, pH 8.0) and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Afterwards, 3.0 μL 10 μM HP and 0.4 μL 5000 U/mL polymerase were added to the solution. Finally, 1.0 μL different DNA sequences, including one-base mutated DNA (1 M), three-bases mutated DNA (3 M), and random sequences were incubated with the prepared mixture at 37 ℃ for another 60 min. The final concentration of these mutated DNA was 0.2 μM. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of each solution was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA detection in aqueous buffer solution and diluted fetal bovine serum
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ability of the sensor in real samples, we use diluted bovine serum as the substrate. The fetal bovine serum samples diluted 100 times by 1xNEB Buffer 2 (50 mM Tris-HCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 100 mM dNTPs, 6% DMSO, and 1 mM DTT, pH 8.0) which first heated at 65 ℃ for 30 min, then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to extract supernatant were used as the determination medium. 2.0 μL 1 μM DNA1 and 2.0 μL 1 μM DNA2-A were added in a 91.6 μL determination medium and incubated at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Subsequently, 3.0 μL 1 μM HP and 0.4 μL 500 U/mL polymerase were introduced to the solution. Then, 1.0 μL target DNA of different concentrations were added into the solution and incubated for another 60 min at 37 ℃. The final concentration of target DNA in the solution were 5.0 and 50.0 nM, respectively. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of each solution was detected. The DNA concentration in real serum was calculated according to the regression equation as determined. The recovery rates were then calculated and compared.

A 30 mL of nondenatured 20% polyacrylamide gel solution was prepared by mixing 3 mL 10 × TBE buffer, 20 mL 30% acrylamide, and 6.7 mL water all together. The solution is stirred vigorously for 1 min. Then, 0.012 mL TEMED was added and the flask was further rotated to ensure that the solution was fully mixed. Afterwards, 0.3 mL fresh 10% (w/v) APS was added. The gel was then polymerized for 30 min at room temperature and the comb and bottom gaskets were pulled out. The lower reservoir of the electrophoresis tank was filled with 1 × TBE buffer. Then, the solution containing 27 μL sample and 3 μL 10 × loading buffer was loaded. The PAGE was performed in 1 × TBE at 5 V/cm for 2 h. Finally, the PAGE gel was stained with SYBR Green solution and the images were taken by the Gel Doc XR System image analysis system (Bio-Rad, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and operation of the established fluorescent DNA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a simple and powerful dual-amplification sensor for DNA detection was established. As shown in Scheme 1 , the detection system consists of two cyclic signal amplification steps (Cycle I and Cycle II). A hairpin probe (HP), a double-stranded DNA (DSD) consisting of DNA1 and DNA2, and polymerase are utilized to fabricate the biosensor. In DSD, DNA1 is modified with a quencher at the 3′-terminus, and DNA2 is modified with a fluorophore at the 5′-terminus. The formation of DSD exhibits an initially quenched fluorescence signal due to the close proximity between the fluorophore and quencher, which permits fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of the target DNA, HP is hybridized with target DNA and then exposes the part of HP ready for DSD binding (i). Then, the HP binds with the end of DNA2 (ii). With the generated blunt 3′-termini, the polymerization reaction in the presence of dNTPs and polymerases (iii) is initiated, which finally frees both the target DNA (iv) and DNA1 (v), as well as a fluorescent signaling DNA product (SDP) containing only the fluorescent tag (v) for fluorescence signal production. The released target DNA then manages to trigger another reaction round, which forms the first amplification layer. Moreover, the displaced DNA1 hybridizes with the HP (vi), which further binds with DNA2 in another DSD to generate the two blunt 3′-termini (vii). With the help of Phi29 polymerase and dNTPs (viii), one more fluorescent SDP is yielded and DNA1 is released again (ix) to trigger the Cycle II. The dual reaction cycles guarantee the significant signal enhancement, thus leading to a remarkably improved detection sensitivity.

It is noted that polymerases are always utilized in hairpin DNA-based, signal-amplifiable nucleic acid assay methods. But they are usually capable of enhancing the response signals in single recycling reaction systems. To be adopted in more advanced sensing systems with dual or even more stages of signal amplification, polymerases usually need to be coupling used with other nucleases, such as nicking enzymes, CRISPR enzymes, ligases, and so on. To our knowledge, this work represents one of the rare cases, in which dual recycling reactions for signal enhancement are achieved by utilizing only one kind of enzyme: polymerases.

To test the interaction events among DNA species and the operation mechanism as shown in Scheme 1, the DNA-triggered, dual-amplified, strand-displacement reaction was studied by gel electrophoresis with DNA1, DNA2, the HP, and target DNA mixing separately or collaboratively. As demonstrated in Fig. 1
A, the lanes 1, 2, 4, and 6, displayed the presence of DNA1, DNA2, the HP, and target DNA, respectively. Compared with lanes 1 and 2, lane 3 for the mixture of DNA1 and DNA2 showed a new band at a relatively slower migration position, which was attributed to the formation of double-stranded DNA (DSD) between DNA1 and DNA2. It is noted that the band for the mixture of DSD (lane 3) was almost the same as that for the HP (lane 4). Lane 5 containing DNA1, DNA2, and the HP exhibited a relatively darker band at similar migration position as those in lanes 3 and 4. This reveals that no interaction between the DSD and HP, which ensures a greatly suppressed background signal in the sensing system. Upon the introduction of the target DNA into the HP solution (lane 7), there was a new bright band with slower mobility compared with that for the HP alone (lane 4). This indicates that the specific recognition and binding event occurred between the HP and target DNA and the target-HP complex was yielded (indicated as the blue arrow). Interestingly, the mixture including DNA1, DNA2, the target DNA, and the HP (lane 8) showed two bright bands, which corresponded to the products of DSD and the target-HP complex, respectively. The presence of polymerases caused an undesired polymerization reaction (lane 9), since there was one more new band with an even slower mobility than that of the target-HP complex. This new band indicated the generation of signaling DNA product (SDP, as shown by the red arrow), and this non-specific strand displacement and DNA extension might induce an unexpected high background signal. Fortunately, the addition of the target DNA into the sample as indicated in lane 9, resulted in an even brighter band in lane 10 than that in lane 9, as depicted by the red arrows. Additionally, the band from the mixture of DSD and HP became vaguer, revealing that the more efficient consumption of DSD and HP in lane 10 than that in lane 9. More importantly, a new band (as shown by the green arrow) appeared at the same migration position as that in lane 1, which clearly confirmed the liberation of DNA1. Both the construction of SDP and the release of DNA1 together guarantees the amplification capability of this proposed sensing system, which may thus lead to an enhanced detection sensitivity. Consequently, the proposed working principle in Scheme 1 was verified.

Furthermore, the fluorescent spectra under different conditions were also recorded and analyzed, as shown in Fig. 1 B. The sample consisting of HP and DSD exhibited a negligible fluorescence intensity (curve c), confirming the occurrence of efficient quenching events within DSD. And the addition of polymerase and dNTPs into the mixture of HP and DSD, there was a slightly enhanced fluorescence signal observed (curve a), which suggests the relative stable co-existence among all the probes and polymerases. On the contrary, a significantly enhanced fluorescence intensity was observed after the introduction of DNA targets (curve a), due to the dual amplification process triggered by the target DNA. The considerable fluorescence growth in the presence of the target DNA was mainly attributed to the recycling use of the target DNA in Cycle I as well as the continuous liberation of DNA1 from DSD in Cycle II via the polymerization processes during the two cyclic reaction rounds, as shown in Scheme 1. The results clearly demonstrates that the dual-amplification capability as designed in the scheme holds a great potential for efficient determination of target DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the DNA detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to achieve the best performance of the developed sensor, some critical factors that might affect the fluorescence signals were systematically studied, and the optimal conditions were selected for subsequent experiments. The examined conditions included the length of DNA2, the ratio between DNA2 and DNA1, the HP concentration, the reaction time, and the concentration of polymerases. First, the length of DNA2 plays a vital role in the formation of DSD, the background signal, and the initiation of the polymerization. DNA2 with different length varying from 24 nt (DNA2A), 26 nt (DNA2B), and 28 nt (DNA2C) were investigated, and both the background and response signals were tested. It is found that the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) reached the largest value when the DNA2A was used ( Fig. 2
A). Furthermore, the fluorescence background highly depends on the quenching efficiency between DNA1 and DNA2. The concentration ratio between DNA2 to DNA1 was carefully studied to obtain the most efficient quenching performance. Since DNA1 served as an amplification trigger in the Cycle II, the free DNA1 should be eliminated to inhibit the non-specific response. Furthermore, DNA2 was the sole fluorescence source, so the concentration of DNA2 was fixed at about 200 nM. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the highest S/N ratio was achieved when the ratio of DNA2:DNA1 was 1:1, which was used in the following experiments.

The HP plays two roles in the amplified biosensing system. First, it binds with target DNA to expose the 3′ end ready for binding with DSD, thereby initiating the Cycle I with the help of polymerases. Second, it hybridizes with the liberated DNA1 to launch the Cycle II amplification reaction. Theoretically speaking, HP will be eventually used up at the end of the amplification system, and determine the fluorescence intensity at the end. Therefore, the concentration of HP was investigated with the concentration of DSD fixed at 200 nM, and the results were shown in Fig. 2 C. It is found that the S/N ratio gradually increased when HP increased from 100 nM to 300 nM and then decreased at 400 nM. The highest S/N ratio was obtained when HP concentration was 300 nM. Consequently, we chose 300 nM HP for the following experiments. Because the proposed biosensing system involved the polymerase-mediated nucleotide incorporation reaction, the reaction time was also examined to obtain the highest fluorescence intensity by detecting 100 nM target DNA. As shown in Fig. 2 D, in the presence of 100 nM target DNA, the fluorescence signal increased dramatically with time, which reached the plateau at about 60 min. The fluorescence growth rate kept at a relatively low level after 60 min. Therefore, 60 min was selected as the suitable reaction time in the developed sensor. Moreover, the impacts of the amount of polymerase on the biosensor was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually when the concentration of Klenow Fragment polymerase increased from 0 U/mL to 20 U/mL and stayed unchanged thereafter. As a result, 20 U/mL Klenow Fragment polymerase was the optimal concentration that was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay performance of the DNA sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the analytical performance of the DNA detection method, target DNA with different concentrations were introduced into the sensing system under the optimized conditions. The results in Fig. 3
A showed the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system with the addition of the different concentrations of target DNA. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced with the increase of the concentration of target DNA, indicating that more DNA1 was released form DSD after the two cyclic amplification processes. The calibration curve of the sensing system towards the concentration of target DNA was demonstrated in Fig. 3 B. The linear range of the sensing system was 0.5 nM to 50.0 nM. The regression equation was Y = 20.56 X + 365.3 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9432, in which Y and X stand for the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the target DNA, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.38 nM according to the 3σ/slope rule. Table 2 demonstrates the performance comparison between the current method with other previously reported nucleic acid testing strategies, which utilized hairpin DNA and polymerase to construct detection schemes with single-, dual-, and even triple-stage signal amplification capability.

Furthermore, the detection selectivity was investigated by recording the response signals upon the introduction of matched and mismatched DNA sequences under the same conditions. Three-bases mismatched (3M), one-base mismatched (1M), and random DNA sequences were used to test the assay selectivity. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensities of the system with three-bases mismatched DNA and random DNA were only 11% of the fluorescence intensity of the target DNA. The sensing system demonstrated an excellent selectivity toward the target DNA over the three-base mismatched DNA and random DNA sequences. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the system with the one-base mismatched DNA was decreased by 28% compared with the target DNA. To further improve the single-base discrimination ability, extra measures should be taken, including the re-design of HP. It is found that the highest fluorescence response was collected only from the sample containing the fully matched DNA sequence, and other mismatched DNA exhibited considerably decreased signals. And the fluorescence signal suppression was proportional to the mismatched base number. The signal comparisons reveal that the proposed sensor had a promising selectivity and managed to identify other analogues.

In order to verify the analytical performance of the sensing system under complex conditions, standard recovery experiments were carried out to test the utility in fetal bovine serum (FBS) samples. Two concentrations of the target DNA (5.0 nM and 50.0 nM) were spiked into the 5% diluted FBS to investigate the recovery rates. The results in Table 3 showed that the recovery rates were 110.0% and 101.4%, respectively. And the relative standard derivations (RSD) were 2.2% and 6.2%, respectively. Both the recovery rate and detection reproducity were satisfactory, which indicated that the developed sensing system performed satisfactorily in the complex biological samples.

In conclusion, a simple and sensitive detection method was developed for DNA analysis and there were two stages of signal amplification mechanisms involved in the proposed method. The fluorescence signal was generated via adjusting the fluorescence quenching and recovery events among DNA probes. And the signal amplification was mainly achieved by the DNA polymerase-mediated strand displacement reactions. The working principle was successfully verified by the gel electrophoresis and fluorescence measurement. The sensing system showed a good detection sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.38 nM. Moreover, the sensing system performed quite well in diluted serum sample, with recovery rates varying from 101.4% to 110.0%. It is noted that the detection selectivity, especially for the single base mismatched DNA should be further upgraded. To sum up, the sensing system holds a promising potential for efficient determination of DNA molecules in real biological samples.

Shuyi He: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Peng Li: Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Le Tang: Software. Mingjian Chen: Data curation. Yuxin Yang: Writing - review & editing. Zhaoyang Zeng: Writing - review & editing. Wei Xiong: Writing - review & editing. Xu Wu: Writing - review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jiahao Huang: Writing - review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant Nos. 21806186, 21975117, 21705076, 31741049, and CSU Innovation-Driven funding (20180026040002), Huxiang High-Level Talent Gathering Project-innovative talents (2019RS1005) and the Research Innovation Program for Graduates of Central South University (1053320190609 and 202211901), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2020JJ4766, 2020JJ4125 and 2018JJ3815).
